children.name: Latrodectus mactans
children.description: Iconic black widow with red hourglass marking. Venom contains alpha-latrotoxin causing severe muscle cramps; fatalities extremely rare with modern care.

children.name: Latrodectus hasselti
children.description: Australia's most medically significant spider; about 2000 envenomations per year. Identifiable by orange-red dorsal stripe. Antivenom available since 1956.

children.name: Latrodectus tredecimguttatus
children.description: Found around the Mediterranean and Central Asia. Historically associated with the tarantism dance phenomenon in southern Italy.

children.name: Latrodectus geometricus
children.description: Cosmopolitan species with distinctive spiked egg sacs. More potent venom than black widow but injects less and is less aggressive.

children.name: Steatoda grossa
children.description: Most common false widow; regularly mistaken for Latrodectus. Widespread in human structures worldwide including Sweden.

children.name: Steatoda nobilis
children.description: Originally from Macaronesia; now established across western Europe. Britain's most feared spider despite very few documented serious bites.

children.name: Spintharus davidattenboroughi
children.description: Described from Jamaica in 2018 alongside 14 other new Spintharus species. Named after David Attenborough to honour his advocacy for the natural world and climate action. The genus is distinguished by a smiley-face pattern on the abdomen. Caribbean Spintharus are a remarkable radiation of visually ornamented cobweb spiders whose colour patterns are thought to function in mate recognition.
